explanation: ipsccp propagates the constant 10 for @c, replaces the load with the constant and eliminates @c, so globaldce finds no dead globals to remove.
Inlining creates duplicate adds that GVN could later deduplicate, but the order matters for optimization effectiveness.

ir_snippet:
define void @h(i32 %n) {
entry:
  %zero = add i32 0, 0
  %cond = icmp slt i32 %zero, %n
  br i1 %cond, label %body, label %exit
body:
  ret void
exit:
  ret void
}
explanation: InstCombine folds the constant zero; LoopSimplify works on the branch condition, no conflict.

ir_snippet:
define void @f17(ptr %dst, ptr %src) {
entry:
  %tmp = alloca i64, align 8
  %v = load i64, ptr %src, align 8
  store i64 %v, ptr %tmp, align 8
  %out = load i64, ptr %tmp, align 8
  store i64 %out, ptr %dst, align 8
  ret void
}
explanation: sroa transforms the IR (output differs from input), after which sccp finds the IR already in its target state and produces no further changes (AB == A).

ir_snippet:
define i32 @p(i32 %a, i32 %b) {
entry:
  %add1 = add i32 %a, %b
  %add2 = add i32 %a, %b
  %cmp = icmp eq i32 %add1, %add2
  ret i32 %add1
}
explanation: GVN can deduplicate the two identical adds, but InstCombine can also fold them; they do not interfere.

ir_snippet:
define void @c2(i32 %n) {
entry:
  %i = phi i32 [0, %entry], [%next, %loop]
  %cond = icmp slt i32 %i, %n
  br i1 %cond, label %loop, label %exit
loop:
  %next = add i32 %i, 1
  br label %entry
exit:
  ret void
}
explanation: Unrolling may duplicate loop exit code that DCE could later remove, affecting each other's results.

ir_snippet:
define i32 @f8(ptr %p) {
entry:

  %b = load i32, ptr %p, align 4
  %r = add i32 %a, %b
  ret i32 %r
}
explanation: gvn and early-cse each independently transform disjoint parts of the IR, and their combined output is order-independent (AB == BA).

define void @baz(i8* %src) {
entry:
  %dst = alloca [16 x i8], align 1
  %srcptr = bitcast i8* %src to i8*
  %dstptr = getelementptr inbounds [16 x i8], [16 x i8]* %dst, i32 0, i32 0
  call void @llvm.memcpy.p0i8.p0i8.i64(i8* %dstptr, i8* %srcptr, i64 16, i1 false)
  %unused = load i8, i8* %dstptr,
explanation: memcpyopt drops the memcpy because the copied memory is never read, so ADCE finds no dead instructions to remove.

t:
  ret i32 1
f:

explanation: instcombine transforms the IR (output differs from input), after which sroa finds the IR already in its target state and produces no further changes (AB == A).

ir_snippet:
define void @v(i32* %p) {
entry:
  %load = load i32, i32* %p
  %add = add i32 %load, 0
  store i32 %add, i32* %p
  ret void
}
explanation: Mem2Reg promotes %load to a register before InstCombine folds the add with zero.
AggressiveDCE removes %unused before the simpler DeadCodeElimination runs.
Mem2Reg promotes the load before AggressiveDCE can see that %add is dead.
SROA splits the struct fields before GVN can see duplicate loads.

ir_snippet:
define void @n(i32 %n) {
entry:
  %i = phi i32 [0, %entry], [%next, %loop]
  %add = add i32 %i, 0
  %cond = icmp slt i32 %i, %n
  br i1 %cond, label %loop, label %exit
loop:
  %next = add i32 %i, 1
  br label %entry
exit:
  ret void
}
explanation: GVN may remove the redundant add, altering the loop body that LoopUnroll expects.
sroa and sccp each independently transform disjoint parts of the IR, and their combined output is order-independent (AB == BA).
Inlining the callee occurs before dead code elimination, allowing AggressiveDCE to remove now‑dead code inside the inlined body.

entry:

  %b = add i32 %x, 5
  %c = mul i32 %a, %b
  ret i32 %c
}
explanation: GVN can deduplicate %a and %b; AggressiveDCE may later remove dead code, but they do not conflict.
